_about: Key teaching claims with their source paper and a note on confidence. Used to build claims_cross_reference.md. Narrative claims were read from clean PDF text; numeric/constant claims flagged where a human should re-check.

claims.claim: All oscillators are linear time-VARIANT (LTV) systems for noise, not LTI: the same noise impulse produces a different phase shift depending on WHEN in the period it is injected.
Amplitude perturbations decay back to the limit cycle (a restoring mechanism is required for stable oscillation), but PHASE perturbations persist indefinitely and accumulate.
claims.source_paper: paper_001, Sec. III-A; paper_004 (APF / decay function)

claims.claim: Phase noise scales as Gamma_rms^2 / q_max^2: increase the signal charge swing q_max and reduce the rms ISF to lower 1/f^2 phase noise.
1/f device noise upconverts to close-in 1/f^3 phase noise ONLY through the DC term c_0 of the ISF; a waveform with good rise/fall symmetry has small c_0 and therefore low 1/f^3 phase noise.
claims.source_paper: paper_001, Eqs (23),(24); paper_002 symmetry section & Fig. 17

claims.claim: The 1/f^3 phase-noise corner is NOT equal to the device 1/f noise corner; it equals omega_1/f * (c_0/Gamma_rms)^2 / 2, so symmetry can push it well below the device corner.

Accumulated (long-term) jitter of a free-running oscillator grows as the square root of the measurement interval: sigma_dt = kappa*sqrt(Delta t). This is the random-walk signature of having no absolute time reference.
For a single-ended ring oscillator at fixed center frequency and power dissipation, phase noise and jitter are essentially INDEPENDENT of the number of stages N.
claims.confidence: medium (conclusion text read; exact constants flagged)

claims.claim: The rms ISF of a single-ended ring scales roughly as N^{-3/2}.

The ISF framework reduces to previously known phase-noise models (e.g. Leeson) as special cases and naturally accommodates cyclostationary noise via an effective ISF Gamma_eff = Gamma * alpha (noise-modulating function).

The same ISF that governs phase noise also governs injection locking/pulling; a single first-order (generalized Adler) equation built from the ISF predicts lock range, locked phase, and stability for arbitrary oscillators and injection waveforms.
claims.confidence: medium (abstract & section text read; equation form flagged)

claims.claim: Amplitude effects under injection are captured by the Amplitude Perturbation Function (APF), the amplitude-domain analog of the ISF (units 1/Ampere); for an ideal LC oscillator the ISF and APF are in quadrature.

The cross-coupled-inverter sense amplifier paper is NOT about oscillator phase noise/ISF; its only conceptual link to this curriculum is the cross-coupled-pair regeneration / positive-feedback mechanism shared with latch-style and LC oscillators.

PPV (perturbation projection vector), adjoint method, and Floquet theory are the rigorous mathematical foundation for the ISF; they come from the broader literature (e.g. Demir-Mehrotra-Roychowdhury 2000, Kaertner), which is NOT among the 5 downloaded PDFs. They are taught here as standard context, clearly labeled as external.
